courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Foundations in Financial Accounting

meetings.location: Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G03
Lee Shau Kee Archi Bldg G03

courses.description: This course develops students' conceptual skills and competence in processing, preparing and reporting accounting information in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP). It introduces the basic concepts and principles of financial accounting including the analysis, recording and reporting of business transactions; preparation and interpretation of financial statements. Topics include the accounting principles, the recording process, accounting for assets, liabilities, net income and shareholders' equity; and decision making using accounting information.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ACCT2111.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:
(1) understand the GAAP underlying the preparation and presentation of financial statements.
(2) apply the appropriate accounting concepts and principles to the recognition and measurement relating to revenues, expenses, assets, liabilities and equity.
(3) undertake the work involved in the recording, processing, summarising and reporting phases of the accounting cycle.
(4) prepare financial statements (statement of financial position, statement of comprehensive income and statement of changes in equity).
(5) appreciate the role and importance of financial accounting information in assisting decision-making in a business context.
(6) apply basic analytical tools to analyse, interpret and report on financial statements.
(7) communicate their analysis and findings in an effective way.
courses.course_syllabus: This course develops students’ conceptual skills and competence in processing, preparing and reporting accounting information in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP). It introduces the basic concepts and principles of financial accounting including the analysis, recording and reporting of business transactions; preparation and interpretation of financial statements. Topics include the accounting principles, the recording process, accounting for assets, liabilities, net income and shareholders’ equity; and decision making using accounting information.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are welcomed to sen their comments/feedback to the course instructor via email or appointment. CTE will be collected at the end of the course.
courses.required_readings: Weygandt, Kimmel and Kieso, Financial Accounting: A Focus on Fundamentals, John Wiley, latest edition.

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Introductory Financial Accounting

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg LT

courses.description: This course is designed to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of financial accounting principles, practices and its underlying theories. In this course, we will emphasize on basic financial accounting concepts and principles, and discuss how to measure a company's net income, assets, liabilities and shareholders' equity using Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP).
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ACCT1111
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students should be able to:
(1) understand the accounting concepts and procedures, and to apply them to the business world
(2) develop skills and increase their ability to solve problems that they will need to succeed in a business environment
(3) learn the technology that allows them access to numerous online materials relating the real world of accounting and business
(4) learn how to study and manage their own tasks that will help them acquire further knowledge on their own
courses.course_syllabus: This course is designed to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of financial accounting principles, practices and its underlying theories. In this course, we will emphasize on basic financial accounting concepts and principles, and discuss how to measure company’s net income, assets, liabilities and shareholders’ equity using Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP). In addition, we will also introduce briefly the development of accounting standards, the international accounting standards (IFRS) and some of the major differences between the GAAP and IFRS.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are welcomed to provide comments and feedback to the instructor via email, appointment, and CTE exercise.
courses.required_readings:
Weygandt, Kimmel and Kieso, Financial Accounting, John Wiley, 6th edition, 2010 with IFRS Update.
ISBN 978-0-470-62327-5
courses.recommended_readings: Jeffrey Waybright, and Robert Kemp, Financial Accounting, Pearson, 2010. ISBN 978-0-13-706779-4

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Introductory Management Accounting
courses.description: This course is designed to provide students with the concepts and techniques of managerial accounting, with the emphasis on the uses of management accounting information by managers in planning, controlling and decisions making. Major topics will include costing systems, activity-based costing, cost-volume-profit relationship, variable costing, budgeting, standard costing, and relevant costs for decision-making.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite: ACCT1111 or ACCT2111
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:
(1) Understand the management accounting concepts and apply the knowledge to both manufacturing and service companies.
(2) Use the information from management accounting systems to improve the competitiveness of the companies in operational excellence, product leadership, and customer service.
(3) Develop skills and ability to solve problems that they will need to succeed in a business environment.
(4) Learn how to study and manage their own tasks that will help them acquire further knowledge on their own.
courses.course_syllabus: This course is an introduction to managerial accounting. It is designed to provide students the concepts and techniques of managerial accounting, with the emphasis on the uses of management accounting information by managers in planning, controlling, and decisions making. The course emphasizes the links between accounting information and management activities such as strategic and operational decision making, financial planning and control, organizational design, and performance evaluation. Major topics will include costing systems, activity-based costing, cost-volume-profit relationship, variable costing, budgeting, standard costing and relevant costs for decision making.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are welcomed to provide comments and feedback to the instructor via email, appointment, and CTE exercise.
courses.required_readings:
Horngren, Data, Foster, Rajan, & Ittner, Cost Accounting – A managerial emphasis, Pearson, 13th edition – the main textbook.
Weygandt, Kimmel, & Kieso, Financial Accounting, Wiley, 6th edition (2010 IFRS update) – for the cash flow statement part only.
courses.recommended_readings: Garrison, Noreen & Brewer, Managerial Accounting, McGraw Hill, 12th edition (International edition).

courses.description: This course seeks to provide an introduction to the legal environment in the business context. To this end, the course will begin with an examination of the legal system of the Hong Kong SAR before proceeding to encompass a selection of topics in laws relating to business such as contract law, law of negligence, employment law, privacy law, laws of equal opportunities, anti-corruption law and anti-competition law. Students can know the important rules of business law. These rules concern how the courts deal with business-related disputes in the real life and students can in particular learn issues about consumers’ rights and to what extent the laws enhance a fair business environment.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ACCT3151
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students will be able to:

(1) have effective written communication skills

(2) acquire the knowledge of selected important rules of / relating to business law

(3) integrate relevant legal knowledge into business decision making

(4) apply the relevant rules to analyze and solve problems in a business setting
courses.course_syllabus:
Topics covered:

1. The legal system of the Hong Kong SAR

2. Contract Law: introduction to formation of contracts, contract terms relating to consumer protection and the Trade Description Ordinance, misrepresentation and remedies for breach of contract

3. Law of Negligence

4. Employment Law

5. Laws of equal opportunities

6. Privacy law

7. Anti-competition law
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are warmly welcomed to send their comments/ feedback to the course instructor. The channels include: (1) e-mails to the instructor; (2) appointment with the instructor; and (3) formal CTE exercise.

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Professional Seminar Series
courses.description: The professional seminars are designed to provide students with an opportunity to meet industry leaders so as to keep abreast of the current development in the accounting and business professions and to explore different career opportunities. Through these seminars, students are expected to have a more in-depth reflection on some current accounting and business issues. Topics of the seminars may range from financial reporting, cost and management accounting, business assurance, taxation to other current business and ethical issues.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By completion of the course, students are expected to be able to:
- keep abreast of the current development in the accounting and business professions;
- have an in-depth reflection on some current accounting and business issues; and
- understand more about various career opportunities in the accounting and business professions.
courses.course_syllabus: Topics of the seminars may range from financial reporting, cost and management accounting, business assurance, taxation to other current business and ethical issues.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are welcomed to provide comments and feedback to the instructor via email and appointment.

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Financial Reporting I
courses.description: This course is designed to augment the knowledge of students who have had instruction in the fundamentals of accounting. To this end, it will provide instruction in accounting theory, the development of accounting principles and their application to income determination, the presentation of financial statements for business, and the management and reporting of assets as well as liabilities.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: ACCT2111
courses.learning_outcomes:
(1) To learn and evaluate current principles of financial accounting.
(2) To apply generally accepted accounting principles to situations involving the recording of transactions and the preparation of financial statements.
(3) To read and critically evaluate financial statements.
(4) To consider the larger context of financial reporting (beyond rules and procedures), including how company executives and financial analysts view financial reporting.
courses.course_syllabus: Accounting theory, development of accounting principles and their applications to income determination and presentation of financial statements for business firms are covered in the course. Measurement and reporting of Assets, Current liabilities and Revenue Recognition are thoroughly discussed. Different applications of accounting rules to the Hong Kong environment are introduced.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Your comments and feedback are welcome addressed to the instructor or teaching assistant any time in the semester through e-mail or personal meeting.
courses.required_readings: Intermediate Accounting, Bazley, Nikolai and Jones, 11th edition, South-Western Publishing Company
courses.recommended_readings:
Reference Book
Intermediate Accounting, Kieso, Weygandt and Warfield, 12th edition, Wiley

HKICPA website
http://www.hkicpa.org.hk

Solution Manual
A set of solution manual will be reserved in library for your convenience. You can check answers after you have practiced the chapter-end questions, exercises, problems.

China Telecommunication Industry Case
China Telecommunication is a listed company in Hong Kong. This case study is to help students to apply various accounting concepts learned in this course to real-world business situations. The case allows students to explore various issues associated with asset valuation. Based on information contained in the case, every student required to develop a critical analysis of the valuation of receivables, fixed assets and intangible assets and submit a written report.

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Business Law
courses.description: This course will provide an introduction to the legal system of Hong Kong with emphasis on the legal environment of business. It will encompass a study of selected topics in contract law including the elements of business contracting, the rights and obligations of parties to the contract, the sale of goods and employment law. This course will also examine the tort of negligence in a professional context.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ACCT2151, LAWS1041 or LAWS1042
courses.learning_outcomes: Students can know the important rules of business law at the end of the course. These rules concern how the courts deal with business-related disputes in the real life. As many of the rules are designed to protect consumers, students can in particular know more about consumers’ rights. In addition, they can learn more analytical skills and writing skills which can help them to apply the rules to do their assessments.
courses.course_syllabus:
This course seeks to provide an introduction to the institutions, doctrines and methodology of the law in the business context. To this end, the course will begin with an examination of the legal system of the Hong Kong SAR before proceeding to encompass a selection of topics in business law such as the law relating to contract, torts, agency, employment and the sale of goods.

The legal system of the Hong Kong SAR

The Basic Law, legislation, common law, equity and the court system

Contract law

Formation of contracts (offer, acceptance, intention to create legal relations, consideration and formality), contract terms and exemption clauses, vitiating factors (misrepresentation, undue influence, illegality and unconscionable contracts), discharge of contracts (performance, agreement and frustration) and the remedies for breach of contract (damages, rescission, injunction and specific performance)

Employment law

Employees / independent contractors and the rights of employees

Torts law

Negligence

Agency law

Teh rights and duties of agens, authority of agents

Sale of goods law

Important rules under the Sale of Goods Ordinance
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are warmly welcome to send email messages to teachers if they have any comment or feedback on the course.

D.K. Srivastava, ed., Business Law in Hong Kong (Hong Kong: Sweet & Maxwell Asia, 2nd ed., 2007)

courses.description: This course is designed to acquaint students with the principles of modern company law in Hong Kong and to increase their awareness of the legal issues which are of interest to the commercial community. It will examine the formation of companies, the effects of incorporation, the division of powers as between the various organs of the company, its relationship with outsiders, key differences in methods of fund raising, options available to companies in financial difficulties and the role of external regulatory bodies.

courses.learning_outcomes: By the end of the course, students will be able to better appreciate the relevance of the legal principles as they apply to modern commercial transactions that are undertaken through the conduit of a company. In addition, students will be apprised of the development of company law in Hong Kong as the government undertakes the Companies Ordinance Rewrite with the objective of enacting a more user friendly and modern regime.

Issues to be examined include the formation of companies, the consequence and effects of incorporation, the division of powers between the various organs of the company, its relationship with outsiders, key differences in methods of fund raising, options available to companies in financial difficulty and the role of external regulatory bodies.

(2) compute the tax payable under different taxes, and
(3) develop basic tax planning techniques

This course will discuss the basic principles of the Hong Kong taxation system. Major topics include salaries tax, property tax, profits tax, personal allowances, personal assessment, and stamp duty.

1. Introduction to the Hong Kong taxation system (Chapter 1)
2. Property Tax (Chapter 6)
3. Salaries tax: scope of charge (Chapter 2)
4. Salaries tax: income from office and employment (Chapter 3)
5. Salaries tax: expenses and deductions, personal allowances and tax computation (Chapter 4)
6. Salaries tax Part IV: husband and wife (Chapter 5)
7. Personal assessment (Chapter 19)
8. Profits tax :scope of charge and meaning of trade (Chapter 9 and chapter 10)
9. Profits tax : receipts (Chapter 11)
10. Profits tax : expenditures (Chapter 12)
11. Profits tax: computation of assessable profit (Chapter 13)
12. Stamp duty (Chapter 20)
13. Objections and appeals (Chapter 8)

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Introduction to Python for Business and Accounting Analytics
courses.description: This introductory course offers a foundational understanding of Python programming as applied in business and accounting analytics. It covers Python programming basics, focusing on data processing and effective visualization techniques relevant to business and financial data. The course emphasizes Python's core elements, including essential syntax, control structures, and methods for managing and analyzing complex, unstructured datasets. Students will engage in practical exercises designed to cultivate skills in utilizing Python for efficient data manipulation, supporting business decision-making and analytics.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite(s): ENGG1003
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:

1. Understand the basics of Python programming logic.
2. Grasp how Python can be applied in the fields of accounting and finance.
3. Conduct basic data processing and cleaning using Python.
4. Create simple visualizations of financial data with Python tools.
5. Apply Python programming skills to assist in basic business decision-making.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to Python in Accounting and Finance
2. Python Programming Basics for Beginners
3. Essential Python Syntax and Control Structures
4. Python Functions, Modules, and Basic Libraries
5. Data Types and Collection Manipulation in Python
6. Basic Data Processing and Cleaning Techniques
7. Introduction to Data Visualization with Python
8. Fundamentals of Financial Data Analysis in Python
9. Handling Unstructured Financial Data in Python
10. Introduction to Financial Forecasting with Python
11. Exploring Python in Crowdsourced Data Analysis
12. Python in Corporate Decision-Making Processes
13. Basics of Machine Learning in Finance with Python
14. Emerging Trends in Python for Accounting and Analytics

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Advanced Financial Accounting
courses.description: This course will deal with the more advanced aspect of financial accounting. Major topics covered will include partnership formation, dissolution and liquidation, mergers and acquisitions, business combinations, equity and cost methods of accounting for subsidiaries, inter-company profit and loss adjustments, special problems in the consolidation of corporate financial statements and foreign currency translation. The different applications of accounting rules to the Hong Kong environment are introduced.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: ACCT3112
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students will be able to:
1. Summarise the different types of business combination and key accounting issues

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Financial Statement Analysis and Valuation
courses.description: The objective of this course is to present a comprehensive and up-to-date treatment of the analysis of financial statement as an aid to decision-making by managers, creditors and shareholders. It will evaluate the properties of accounting policies and estimates of a firm and will assess a firm's performance using tools such as ratio analysis and cash flow analysis. The role of accounting information in capital market and contracting process will also be examined. Students are expected to complete a project analysing the financial statements of companies.
courses.enrollment_requirement: 1. Prerequisite: ACCT3112 or permission from instructor
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course students should have answers to the following questions:

• How are fundamental values (or “intrinsic values”) estimated?

• How are business strategies analyzed in order to understand the value they create?

• How is “value created for shareholders” identified?

• How does one pull apart the financial statements to get at the relevant information for valuing equities?

• What is the relevance of cash-flows? Of dividends? Of earnings? Of book values?

• How does one handle the imprecision in valuation models?

• How does ratio analysis help in valuation?

• How does reverse engineering work in stock selection?

• How does profitability tie into valuation?

• What is growth? How does one analyze growth? How does one value a growth firm?

• How does one analyze the quality of financial reports?

• How does one deal with the accounting methods used in financial statements?

• How is financial analysis developed for strategy and planning?

• What determines a firm's P/E ratio? How does one calculate what the P/E should be?

• What determines a firm's market-to-book (P/B) ratio? How does one calculate what the P/B should be?

• How does one evaluate risk? For equity? For debt?

• How does one evaluate an equity research report?

• How does one trade on fundamental information?
courses.course_syllabus:
This course is about the analysis of financial information - particularly firms’ financial statements - for making decisions to invest in businesses. The primary focus is on equity (share) valuation. When buying a share in a firm, the investor wants to know what a reasonable price is to pay: What is the firm worth? The course explains how he or she answers that question by analyzing information. As financial statements contain considerable information about firms, the course involves financial tatement analysis. The methods of fundamental analysis will be covered in detail and applied in cases and projects involving

listed companies. Topics include models of shareholder value, a comparison of accrual accounting and discounted cash flow approaches to valuation, reverse engineering methods of stock selection, the analysis of profitability, growth and aluation generation in a firm, diagnosing accounting quality, forecasting earnings and cash flows, pro-forma analysis for strategy and planning, and the determination of price/earnings and market-to-book ratios. The course does not “assume market efficiency.” Rather, it develops the techniques for challenging traded prices in order to assess whether investments are appropriately priced.

Content, highlighting fundamental concepts

The course will be taught with a view to designing techniques to be used in an (independent) equity research department or firm. Accordingly, the material is of particular interest to buyand sell- side equity analyst who must issue a buy, hold or sell recommendations on stocks. However, much of the material covered in the course is also relevant to the corporate financial analyst and for private equity analysis, for evaluating acquisitions, restructurings and other investments, and for calculating the value generated by strategic scenarios. By the end of the course, the student should feel competent in writing a thorough, credible equity research report or investment analysis that meets the highest standards of professionalism. The course is of interest to those contemplating careers in investment banking (particularly in the equity area), security analysis, consulting, private equity, public accounting, and corporate finance. And it will also help with personal investing. The course will be in the form of lectures, discussions, exercises, and cases, and will involve a course project.

courses.title: Advanced Financial Accounting and Reporting
courses.description: This course deals with advanced aspects of financial accounting. Topics covered include the financial reporting environment in Hong Kong, fair value accounting, intangible assets, contingent liabilities, deferred income taxes, accounting for post-retirement benefits, equity and cost methods of accounting for long-term investments, business combinations (purchase and pooling), consolidation of parent/subsidiary financial statements, and interpretation of financial statements. Students will be required to critically evaluate various accounting valuation, reporting and disclosure issues.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Accountancy
courses.course_syllabus:
- Share-based Payment
- Accounting for Taxes on Income
- Accounting for the Effects of Change in Foreign Exchange Rates
- Derivatives and Hedge Accounting Part I
- Derivatives and Hedge Accounting Part II
- Group Reporting I: Concept
- Group Reporting II: Acquisition Method under IFRS 3
- Group Reporting III: Accounting for Business Combination and Non-controlling
- Interest in Post-acquisition Period under IFRS 3
- Group Reporting IV: Consolidation under IFRS 10 Part I
- Group Reporting IV: Consolidation under IFRS 10 Part II
- Group Reporting V: Equity Accounting under IAS 28
- Special Issues on Consolidation: Deferred Tax
- Effects on Consolidation, Step Acquisitions
courses.feedback_evaluation: Mandatory Course and Teaching Evaluation (CTE) is conducted at the end of the course.
courses.required_readings: P. Tan, L.C. Yeoung, and K.E. Wen, Advanced Financial Accounting: An IFRS Approach, 4th Edition, McGraw Hill.

courses.description: This course provides a graduate-level overview of the principles and concepts that underlie corporate financial reporting and the economic consequences of accounting choices. The course focus is on developing the skills required to interpret the information contained in financial statements and its use in decision-making rather than on the skills required to prepare financial statements. Topics include the use of accounting information in evaluating firm performance, and corporate reporting policies.

courses.description: The overall goal of this course is to provide students with the knowledge to understand, evaluate, and act on financial and non-financial information used in managing contemporary organizations. It considers two distinct perspectives of users of accounting information: external and internal users. In particular, the course introduces basic concepts of financial and managerial accounting and presents commonly-used analytical tools and techniques to help with decision-making. Such decisions cover a broad range of user objectives such as investing, lending, assessing competitiveness, pricing of products, costing, inventory management, performance measurement, etc.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Management
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students are expected to:
- differentiate between assessing a company from the outside and measuring performance from an internal perspective
- analyze and critically judge the financial and non-financial performance of an enterprise using financial statements
- understand modern concepts of internal resource management, revenue generation, cost behavior, and ultimately performance evaluation through profitability and non-financial metrics
courses.course_syllabus:
• Introduction to the accrual concept; accrual and cash accounting
• Basic financial statements and financial ratios
• Income statement; revenue recognition, expenses, profits; the matching principle
• Balance sheet; assets, liabilities, and shareholders’ equity; the accounting equation
• Cash flow statement; operating, investing, and financing activities
• Introduction to cost concepts; allocating costs and activity-based costing, the cost-volume-profit (CVP) relationship
• Planning and analyzing performance: budgeting, standard costing and variance analysis
• Assessing performance in the context of strategy; People-Service-Profit and the Balanced Scorecard
• Decentralization, transfer pricing, and multinational considerations
• Performance evaluation, incentives and behavioral issues
courses.feedback_evaluation: Mandatory Course and Teaching Evaluation (CTE) is conducted at the end of the course.
courses.required_readings: The course is intended to provide a quick summary of the gist of two otherwise full courses in accounting. Thus, its structure does not follow any standard textbook. As a result, there is no required textbook for the course.
courses.recommended_readings:
“Financial and Managerial Accounting for MBAs,” by Peter Easton, Robert Halsey, Mary Lea McAnally, Al Hartgraves, Dale Morse Wayne Morse
“Financial Accounting”
“Management/Managerial Accounting”

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Cost and Management Accounting
courses.description: This course introduces the students to the basic concepts, techniques and application of cost and management accounting. Topics include cost concepts, cost behaviour, costing systems, cost determination and allocation, standard costing and variance analysis, relevant costs, and budgetary planning and performance evaluation. Methods and techniques of cost management in the service sector and in the advanced manufacturing environment are discussed. Accounting implications for management planning, control, and decision-making will be emphasized. Throughout this course, students will be expected to actively participate in class seminars case discussions.

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Introductory Accounting Data Analytics
courses.description: This course is designed to prepare students with the necessary tools and skills they need to successfully perform data analytics. The course requires students to have basic financial and managerial accounting knowledge. Students will learn about using data analytics to ask and answer accounting and business questions, preparing data for analysis, performing different types of data analysis using data analysis tools such as Excel and Tableau. Finally, students will learn how to communicate the analysis results effectively using different types of visualizations. Hands-on computer laboratory sessions will form an integral part of this course.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Accountancy
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, students should be able to:
1. use data analytics to ask and answer accounting and business questions
2. perform extract, transform and load (ETL) procedures for data preparation and cleaning
3. perform descriptive, diagnostic, predictive and prescriptive analysis related to financial reporting, management accounting, auditing and financial statements analysis independently
4. communicate the analysis results effectively using appropriate visualizations

courses.description: This course introduces students to the institutions, doctrine and methodology of the law in the business and corporation contents. After a discussion of the legal system of Hong Kong SAR, the first part of the course examines selected subjects in contract, tort of negligence, employment, and sales of goods. The second part of the course will focus on company law, such as formation of companies, the effects of incorporations, the division of powers and obligations among various organs of the company, its relationship with outsiders, financing and dividend issues, and options available to companies in financial difficulties.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Accountancy
courses.learning_outcomes: Students can know the important rules of business law and company law at the end of the course. These rules concern how the courts deal with business-related disputes and issues relating to the running of companies in the real life. As many of the rules are designed to protect consumers and shareholders, students can in particular know more about consumers’ rights and shareholders’ rights. In addition, they can also learn more analytical skills and writing skills which can help them to apply the rules to do their assessments.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. The legal system of the Hong Kong SAR
• The Basic Law, legislation (including the anti-bribery law and laws against money laundering), common law, equity and the court system
2. Contract law
• Formation of contracts (offer, acceptance, intention to create legal relations, consideration and formality), contract terms and exemption clauses, vitiating factors (misrepresentation, undue influence, illegality and unconscionable contracts), discharge of contracts (performance, agreement and frustration) and the remedies for breach of contract (damages, rescission, injunction and specific performance)
3. Torts law
• Negligence
4. Company law
• Business organizations and incorporation, the external regulatory bodies, the constitution of a company, the raising of capital and the maintenance of share capital, the management of a company, the dissolution of a company and solutions for companies having financial difficulties
5. Employment and agency laws
• Employees / independent contractors and the rights of employees, and agency law principles
courses.feedback_evaluation: Mandatory Course and Teaching Evaluation (CTE) is conducted at the end of the course.
courses.required_readings:
1. D.K. Srivastava, ed., Business Law in Hong Kong (Hong Kong: Sweet & Maxwell Asia, 6th ed., 2020)
2. Vanessa Stott, Hong Kong Company Law (Hong Kong: Person Education Asia Limited, 15th ed., 2020)
courses.recommended_readings: Stefan HC Lo and Charles Qu, Law of Companies in Hong Kong (Hong Kong: Sweet & Maxwell, 3rd ed., 2018)

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Fundamental Accounting – Financial Statement Analysis
courses.description: The objective of this course is to provide an in-depth analysis of the requirement and practice of financial reporting and financial disclosure in mainland China and listed Chinese companies, including A-share, B-share, and H-share firms. The course consists of four parts: 1) the legal and regulatory framework; 2) the reporting and disclosure process; 3) issues in analysing IPO prospectus; and 4) issues in analysing annual/interim reports. The course will be a mix of lectures, class discussion, and cases.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MBA in Finance (40087); or MBA in Finance (40324); or MBA in Technology and Innovation
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students will:
- be able to read financial statements
- understand the principles of drafting financial statements
- be able to understand how external environment and internal factors influence the value relevance of accounting information of enterprises
- be able to apply economic intuition and dynamic method to analyse the effect of enterprise management, investment and financing strategies on accounting information
courses.course_syllabus:
This course will fully describe the basic conceptual framework and analytical theoretical system of financial reporting. The focus of course content design is to help students build an effective theoretical framework for financial analysis based on the understanding of accounting concepts. We must not only understand the meaning of accounting concepts within the framework of the standards, but also have a deep understanding of the impact of internal corporate governance arrangements and the external institutional environment on accounting information. In the teaching process, we will not ignore the fraud in the company's financial reporting practice, and summarize the frequently used financial fraud sections of financial reporting entities to improve the effectiveness of financial analysis. The course will also combine financial analysis with alternative data analysis, and introduce the application of emerging technologies in the field of financial analysis, to help students re-examine financial analysis with a broader perspective.

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Contemporary Management Accounting
courses.description: This course examines the use of contemporary accounting systems for cost measurement, control, performance evaluation, and as a source of sustainable competitive advantage. The course begins with discussion of cost concepts and traditional accounting control and costing systems and then proceeds to examination of contemporary accounting systems including activity-based systems for product pricing, cost control and customer profitability management, and EVA and Balanced Scorecard systems for strategic control and performance evaluation. Practical exposure to the use of these systems in decision-making and the issues involved in their design and implementation are provided through the use of case analysis, current research and student projects. Prerequisite: ACCT5111

courses.learning_outcomes: Upon completion of this course, students will be able to use accounting information in decision analysis and control, including cost analysis and control, profit planning, budgeting, variance analysis, responsibility accounting, and incentive system design and implementation.

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Business Valuation and Analysis
courses.description: This course introduces a framework for business valuation analysis using corporate disclosure and financial statement data. The framework identifies four key components of valuation: business strategy analysis, accounting analysis, financial analysis, and prospective analysis. Employing a case methodology, applications of this framework in a variety of decision-making contexts including securities analysis, credit analysis, and merger and acquisition decisions are examined.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Accountancy; or Executive Master of Professional Accountancy; or MBA; or JD/MBA Double Degree Programme; or MBA in Finance; or MBA in Technology and Innovation
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, students should be able to:
a) Develop a comprehensive framework for business analysis and valuation by identifying and utilizing value relevant information provided in the financial statements
b) Analyze accounting policies, evaluate earnings quality and make necessary adjustments to financial statements
c) Apply appropriate financial tools to perform critical analysis on firm’s profitability, liquidity, solvency and asset utilization
d) Formulate sound financial decisions under context-specific settings by incorporating the strategic, accounting and financing activities of a business entity into the performance/valuation analysis
e) Show the competency in writing a thorough, credible and professional financial statement analysis report
courses.course_syllabus:
• Overview of Financial Statement Analysis and Information Collection
• Understanding the Business
• Accounting Analysis
• Financial Ratio Analysis
• Cash Flow Analysis
• Structured Forecasting
• Forecasting Details
• The Cost of Capital

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Comparative Financial Reporting
courses.description: This course examines the evolution of accounting under different political and economic systems and explores current theory and research on the impact of local institutional features on financial reporting and disclosure practices. In so doing, it provides students with a conceptual framework for analyzing the economic causes and consequences of differences in reporting and disclosure practices and their impact on international commerce, finance, and investment. The course includes discussion of recent and proposed changes in reporting standards with a focus on the local and Mainland reporting environment.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Accountancy; or Executive Master of Professional Accountancy; or MBA; or JD/MBA Double Degree Programme
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, students should be able to:
- Understand the major differences among IFRS, US GAAP and China Accounting Standards, and the progress in their convergences.
- Understand the most recent accounting development in the world and be familiar with important new accounting standards.
- Understand the economic consequences of accounting policies and choices from the perspectives of investors, managers, and government regulators.
- Improve the ability of making good judgment to complex business transactions.

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Fraud Examination and Forensic Investigation
courses.description: Fraud examination and forensic investigation is a growing area of practice in which the knowledge, skills and abilities of advanced accounting are combined with investigative expertise and applied to legal problems. This course covers the important underpinnings as well as the substance of forensic investigation of fraudulent activities such as financial statement frauds, cybercrime activities, corruption and money laundering schemes. The course also explores how analytics tools help forensic accountants to maximise auditing and analytic capabilities.

The objectives of the course are to:

2. let students understand how a forensic accountant can assist top management in preventing, detecting and correcting frauds; and
3. strengthen students’ awareness of how technology helps respond to fraud risks from a technical and practical perspective.

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Accounting Information Systems and Internal Control
courses.description: This course consists of two related areas. This first part analyzes information systems from a management control perspective, emphasizing organization environment, technology, decision models and performance evaluation as determinants of information processing requirements; using cases and design projects to explore the management of information processing systems, major functional applications and impacts of information technology on individuals and society. The second part introduces the systems, procedures, and processes management employed to control operating activities, information reporting systems, and compliance with applicable laws, rules and regulations. Topics include design and evaluation perspectives of control environment, risk assessment, control activities, information and communication and monitoring.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Executive Master of Professional Accountancy; or Master of Accountancy
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, students will be able to:
(1) Understand the relationship between information systems, internal control and enterprise risk management.
(2) Recognize the relationship between general control, application control, and information system construction in contemporary information accounting systems.
(3) Understand the theory and structure of relational databases. And learn how to use relational databases. And through data modeling technology, combined with business models and operating policies to establish a complete accounting information system. At the same time, it can effectively use the concept of relational database and the structure of a relational database to strengthen the internal control of the accounting information system.
(4) After an in-depth understanding of revenue cycle operating policies and business processes, discuss how the chief financial officer, accountants, and internal auditors can design accounting information systems within the corporate revenue cycle and build a complete internal control system.
(5) Discuss the relationship and core technologies of the Internet and cryptography.
(6) Discuss cryptography, the correlation and core technology of blockchain and smart contracts.
(7) Discuss the correlation and core technology of big data, quantum computing, and artificial intelligence.

Topic 3: Accounting system design: relational database and information system construction
Topic 4: Accounting information system and internal control: revenue cycle

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Accounting Data Analytics and Business Intelligence
courses.description:
While the production of data is expanding at an astonishing pace, companies are on average using only a fraction of the data out there. Recent survey by IMA (Institute of Management Accountants) in 2016 reveals significant skill gaps in many areas employers need most, especially identifying key data trends, data mining and extraction, operational analysis, technological acumen, statistical modeling and data analysis. This course targets towards accounting students who are interested in getting hands-on experience with data analytics and rising to the challenges and opportunities big data presents to the profession.

The course will provide a basis for handling and analyzing large-scale data stored within and outside an organization and finding business insights from analyses. In the first two-thirds of the course, we will study the basic concepts and techniques related to data preprocessing, data exploration, model building and results interpretation. Instead of digging deep into technical details, the course will provide a broad survey of common approaches and focus on the pragmatic implementation of data analytics. In the latter one-third of the course, we will discuss the emerging trends in big data and their applications in the accounting profession as well as other industries. The course will try to utilize real datasets related to accounting and finance and enable students to “learn by doing” through hands-on experience with data.

Prerequisites:
Students who take the course need a basic background of statistics. Having a passion for learning new things is important for the course.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Accountancy and Executive Master of Professional Accountancy.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking the course, students should:
(1) Understand the basic concepts and techniques for data analytics
(2) Learn to approach business problems data-analytically
(3) Be able to choose the appropriate tools and methods for different data analysis tasks at hand and implement simple tasks in data analysis, with an understanding of their limitations
(4) Be capable of interacting competently on the topic of data analytics and collaborating with other colleagues in a data analysis project
(5) Be prepared to deal with new trends in analytics techniques affecting businesses and seize the future opportunities brought by “big data” to the accounting profession
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction & Review of Statistics
2. Data Manipulation and Preprocessing
3. Descriptive Analytics & Visualization
4. Unsupervised Learning
5. Predictive Analytics - Linear Regression
6. Predictive Analytics - Classification
7. Advanced Supervised Learning / Causal Inference
8. Web Scraping and Textual Analysis
9. Emerging Technologies
10. Big Data and Its Applications

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Blockchain Technology for Accounting
courses.description:
This course aims to provide students with fundamental yet comprehensive knowledge of blockchain technology. Students who wish to explore what blockchain technology’s potential applications are and how they can change the accounting industry are encouraged to take this course. By the end of the course, students are expected to appreciate and reflect on their role as a professional accountant and be ready for the upcoming transition to the new business environment.

In the first building block, we introduce the history and fundamentals of blockchain technology. Although technical, this part is essential to establish a foundation to understand the capabilities and limitations of blockchain technology. The programming assignment will give practical experience of interacting with blockchain technology.

In the second building block, we discuss the practical applications of blockchain technology, such as how to buy, mine, store, and use cryptocurrencies, enterprise applications of blockchain (e.g., decentralized applications (DApps) and decentralized finance (DeFi)), and the future of artificial intelligence (AI) and metaverse. Industry experts and entrepreneurs will give guest lectures on the real-world blockchain applications and interact with students.

In the final building block, we discuss the accounting policies related to valuing and accounting for various crypto assets. We will also discover the regulatory risk and opportunities of the crypto markets in Hong Kong, Mainland China, and the US. The course concludes by exploring how blockchain technology can shape the future landscape of the accounting industry.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Accountancy
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:

* Understand the basic concepts and technologies of blockchain
* Understand and evaluate various blockchain applications and how those would affect accounting and auditing practices
* Understand and appreciate how blockchain technology has the potential to impact various aspects of our society, and how it can open the door to many entrepreneurial opportunities

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: International Financial Management and Investment
courses.description:
While the global economies and financial markets are increasingly integrated, the tariff and trade war is developing and geopolitical risks are becoming more intensive. The aggressive fiscal, monetary, and industrial policies are also bringing very high uncertainty for the cross-border trade and investment of firms and investment institutions. They are eager for cross-border operations and investment, but in doing so they are also more exposed to the risks of supply chain restructuring and investment.

Combining practices and cases of firms and investment institutions under this background, this course aims to guide students through learning open economy, exchange rate and investment theories and principles, analysing how theories are applied to operations of the international monetary and capital markets, and understanding the strategies, frameworks and methods firms must possess in undertaking financial management, investment, mergers and acquisitions.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Executive Master of Professional Accountancy
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Macroeconomic and Financial Linkages
US Internal and External Balance and Debt Problem; China Macro Financial Policy Trade-Off; US Tariff and Trade War; Global Supply Chain Restructuring
2. International Monetary Systems
US Financial Power and Exceptionalism; Financial Sanctions; International Monetary System Fragmentation and RMB Internationalization; Digital Currencies
3. Cross-Border Asset Liability Management and Carry Trade
International Fisher Equation; the Purchasing Power Parity; Interest Rate Parity; Carry-Trade; Cross-Border Asset Liability Management
4. Exchange Rate Determination: Theories and Practices
The Monetary Approach and Portfolio Balance Approach; US, BRICs Countries, and HK Exchange Rate Systems and Determination
5. Cross-Border Exchange Rate and Interest Rate Risk Management
Exchange Rate Risk Classification; Interest Rate Risk Impacts; Management Costs, Strategies, and Instruments
6. Global Asset Allocation
Asset Allocation Frameworks and Optimization Methods; Wealth Management Targets; Country and Asset Diversification; Strategic Cross-Cycle Allocation and Tactic Allocation
7. Financial Market Investment Management
Passive and Active Investing; Investment Portfolio Management; Relative value of Main Markets and Industries; Relative Value of Main Asset Classes
8. Cross-Border Mergers and Acquisitions
Global Mergers, Acquisitions, and Direct Investment Under Geopolitical Uncertainty; M&A Methods; Value Creation; Success and Failure Cases

a) Understand the relationship between development and value creation of a company and the role of strategic management for a company’s development
b) Make use of various kinds of tools to analyze and propose strategy
c) Enhance their capability to communicate as a financial manager with colleagues of other divisions with regard to corporate strategy
courses.course_syllabus:
- What is a strategy? Why is a strategy needed? What kind of strategy is needed? How to develop a strategy?
- Basic analysis of strategic planning
- Strategic Elements: Diamond Model
- Implementation of the strategy
- Accounting, Finance and Corporate Strategy
courses.feedback_evaluation: Mandatory Course and Teaching Evaluation (CTE) is conducted at the end of the course.
courses.required_readings:
1. Course lecture notes
2. 迈克尔．希特、杜安．爱尔兰、罗伯特．霍斯基森，《战略管理——竞争与全球（概念）》，第12 版，赵宏霞等译，机械工业出版社
courses.recommended_readings:
1. 迈克尔．波特 (Michael E.Porter)，《竞争优势》(Competitive Advantage: Creating and Sustaining Superior Performance)，陈丽芳 (译)，中信出版社，2014 年 6 月 1 日。
2. 迈克尔．波特 (Michael E.Porter)，《竞争战略》(Competitive Strategy: Techniques for Analyzing Industries and Competitors)， 陈丽芳 (译)，中信出版社，2014 年 7 月 1 日。
3. 加里．哈默，比尔．布林著，《管理的未来》，中信出版社，2012 年 9 月第 2 版。
4. 戴维．贝赞可 (David Besanko)，《战略经济学（第五版）》(Economics of Strategy)，中国人民大学出版社，2015 年 2 月 1 日。
5. 菲利普．科特勒、凯文．莱恩．凯勒，《营销管理（第15版．彩色版）》，何佳讯 (译)，格致出版社，2016年 8 月 1 日。
6. W．钱．金 (作者)，勒妮．莫博涅《蓝海战略：超越产业竞争，开创全新市场（扩展版）》(Blue Ocean Strategy How to Create Uncontested Market Space and Make the Competition Irrelevant)，吉宓 (译)，商务印书馆，2016 年 4 月 1 日。
7. 劳伦斯．弗里德曼，《战略：一部历史》(Oxford University Press 2013)，王坚、马娟娟译，社会科学文献出版社，2013 年。
8. Chris Anderson，《长尾理论：为什么商业的未来是小众市场》(The Long Tail: Why The Future of Business is Selling Less of More)，第四版，中信出版集团股份有限公司，2015 年 8 月 1 日。
9. 曾鸣著，《智能商业》，中信出版社，2018 年 11 月第 1 版。

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Managerial Economics and Business Strategy for Accounting Managers
courses.description: Rational and foresight business strategies will help firms to succeed in a competitive market, and to strengthen their capability to develop continuously. This course is based on the basic theory and framework of Managerial Economics, particularly designed for CFO, through easy and readable language and business cases, introducing and demonstrating the heart of Managerial Economics to help CFOs to make successful business strategies.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Executive Master of Professional Accountancy
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the subject, students will be able to:
a) Sort out the crucial elements for decision-making from a complex context of business
b) Understand the basic principles of game theory and how these principles could be applied
c) Think from the opponents perspectives in order to make the best strategy which may ultimately be conducive to a win-win situation for all stakeholders
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to Game Theory
2. Simultaneous game
3. Infinite repetition game
4. Limited repeated game
5. Multi-stage game
6. Game application under asymmetric information
courses.feedback_evaluation: Mandatory Course and Teaching Evaluation (CTE) is conducted at the end of the course.
courses.required_readings:
• Lecture Notes
• 《妙趣横生博弈论》，阿维纳什．迪克西特（Michael R. Baye），机械工业出版社
• 《博弈与社会讲义》，张维迎，北京大学出版社

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Issues in Financial Accounting
courses.description: Current research topics in financial accounting. These include accounting information and capital market behaviour, the economic consequences of both mandatory and voluntarily public announcements made by firms, effects of agency costs and debt convenants on the behaviour of firms’ reporting choices, fundamental analysis of accounting data, compensation schemes and financial reporting.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Accountancy
courses.learning_outcomes:
By completion of the course, students will be able to:
L1: Master basic financial accounting theories
L2: Develop a good understanding of the evolution of financial accounting research
L3: Develop basic computing skills in empirical accounting research
L4: Fluently communicate research ideas in a well-structured approach
L5: Assess the validity of research work in financial accounting
L6: Develop a basic work framework for generating new research ideas
courses.course_syllabus:
Empirical financial accounting topics focusing on capital markets and the use of accounting information in various contracting settings:

1. Introduction and basic earnings-stock price relationship

2. Properties of accounting numbers

3. Measuring earnings characteristics

4. Early life-cycle: pre-IPO, IPO

5. Bankruptcy

6. Disclosure

7. Litigation

8. Auditors

9. Financial analysts

10. Corporate social responsibility

11. Social media

12. International issues
courses.feedback_evaluation: Mandatory course and teaching evaluation (CTE) is conducted at the end of the course
courses.required_readings: To be provided by the course teacher
courses.recommended_readings:
* Teaching materials to be provided by the course lecturer(s).
* To be provided by course instructor.

courses.subject: ACCT
courses.title: Issues in Managerial Accounting
courses.description: Current research topics in managerial accounting. These include analysis of information values, theory of syndicates and teams; organization form and hierarchy, advanced topics in cost allocations and transfer pricing, decision models in management accounting, management accounting for multi-national corporations, agency issues in executive contracts, contingency theory and design of management accounting systems, behavioural dimensions of management accounting.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Accountancy
courses.learning_outcomes:
By completion of the course, students will be able to:
L1: Master basic managerial accounting theories
L2: Develop a good understanding of the evolution of managerial accounting research
L3: Develop basic modelling skills in theoretical accounting research
L4: Fluently communicate research ideas in a well-structured approach
L5: Assess the validity of research work in managerial accounting
L6: Develop a basic work framework for generating new research ideas in managerial accounting
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to management accounting research

2. Management control systems

3. Interfirm relations

4. Performance goals and targets

5. Tournament incentives

6. Non-cash rewards

7. Performance measurement

8. Subjectivity and discretion

9. Implicit incentives and norms

10. Feedback, personnel and cultural controls, recognition
